input.prompt:
I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make
this page theme-aware in XAML and explain the C# alternative.

I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and

input.initialPrompt:
I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make
this page theme-aware in XAML and explain the C# alternative.

data.content:
Here's how to make your page theme-aware:

## XAML â€” `AppThemeBinding`

```xml
<ContentPage xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/dotnet/2021/maui"
             xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2009/xaml"
             x:Class="MyApp.MainPage"
             BackgroundColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#1E1E1E}">

    <VerticalStackLayout Padding="20" Spacing="16">
        <Label Text="Welcome!"
               FontSize="28"
               TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#000000, Dark=#E0E0E0}" />

        <Label Text="This page follows system theme."
               FontSize="16"
               TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#444444, Dark=#BBBBBB}" />

        <Button Text="Get Started"
                BackgroundColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#6200EE, Dark=#BB86FC}"
                TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#000000}" />
    </VerticalStackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

`AppThemeBinding` reacts to `Application.Current.RequestedTheme` automatically â€” no event wiring needed. When the OS theme changes, every bound property updates in real time.

## C# Alternative â€” `SetAppTheme`

The equivalent in code-behind or C# markup:

```csharp
// Extension method on any BindableObject
myLabel.SetAppTheme(Label.TextColorProperty,
    Colors.Black,   // Light value
    Color.FromArgb("#E0E0E0")); // Dark value

BackgroundColor = Application.Current!.RequestedTheme == AppTheme.Dark
    ? Color.FromArgb("#1E1E1E")
    : Colors.White;
```

`SetAppTheme<T>()` is the programmatic equivalent of `AppThemeBinding` â€” it registers a listener on the same theme-change mechanism.

## Using Resource Dictionaries (recommended for larger apps)

Define colors once in `App.xaml` and reference them everywhere:

```xml
<!-- App.xaml -->
<Application.Resources>
    <Color x:Key="PageBg">
        <!-- Can't use AppThemeBinding on Color directly;
             use AppThemeBinding at the consumption site instead -->
    </Color>
</Application.Resources>
```

A cleaner pattern is pairing `AppThemeBinding` with **two merged dictionaries**:

```xml
<!-- LightTheme.xaml / DarkTheme.xaml as ResourceDictionaries -->
<!-- Then in App.xaml.cs: -->
```
```csharp
Application.Current.RequestedThemeChanged += (s, e) =>
{
    // Swap merged dictionaries if using that pattern
};
```

**Key points:**
- `AppThemeBinding` is the simplest approach â€” use it inline for small apps.
- `SetAppTheme()` is the C# equivalent for code-behind or dynamic UI.
- For larger apps, centralize colors in resource dictionaries and reference them by `{StaticResource}` or `{DynamicResource}` key.
- Both approaches listen to OS theme changes automatically with no manual event subscription required.
